chunk_summary: This section discusses diagnostic anchors and differential diagnosis for eating disorders in adolescents, emphasizing early recognition, DSM-5 categories, cultural variations, and red flags for severe physiologic compromise.
learning_objectives: Understand DSM-5 diagnostic categories for eating disorders in adolescents.
Identify cultural and gender-specific presentations of eating disorders.
Recognize red flags and differentiate eating disorders from other causes of adolescent weight loss.
Apply a broad differential diagnosis to adolescent weight loss cases.
key_facts: DSM-5 categories include anorexia nervosa, bulimia nervosa, binge-eating disorder, ARFID, and atypical anorexia nervosa.
Red flags include rapid weight loss, bradycardia, orthostatic symptoms, amenorrhea, lanugo, and unexplained electrolyte abnormalities.
Normal weight does not exclude risk, especially in atypical anorexia nervosa.
Cultural presentations may include 'clean eating,' religious fasting, or sport-driven physique demands.
Differential diagnosis includes malignancy, TB, IBD, endocrine disorders, major depression, substance use, and food insecurity.
reference_quote: Early recognition of eating disorders prevents severe physiologic compromise. Maintain a broad differential for adolescent weight loss, including malignancy, TB, IBD, endocrine disease, and food insecurity. (AAP Clinical Report)
document_key_topics: Red flags for severe physiologic compromise
Differential diagnosis for adolescent weight loss
